coredata.dc:title: Amorphization in mechanically alloyed (Ti, Zr, Nb)–(Cu, Ni)–Al equiatomic alloys
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2006 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, L.C.
Kim, K.B.
Yu, P.
Zhang, W.Y.
Kunz, U.
Eckert, J.
coredata.dc:description: Phase formation by mechanical alloying in (Ti, Zr, Nb)–(Cu, Ni)–Al equiatomic alloys has been investigated. The structural features and the thermal stability were characterized by X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscopy and differential scanning calorimetry. An amorphous phase forms when the (Ti, Zr, Nb) content is between 20 and 60at.%. In contrast, solid solutions of Cu and (Nb, Zr) phase dominate in the final product with (Ti, Zr, Nb) content of 10 and ≥70at.%, respectively. All amorphous phases have a large supercooled liquid region of over 125K, indicating a high thermal stability against crystallization. Miedema's model is used to calculate the glass-forming range in this multicomponent alloy system. The calculated region coincides well with the experimental results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amorphous
Equiatomic
Mechanical alloying
Thermal stability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the (Ti, Zr, Nb)50(Cu, Ni)40Al10 powder mixtures after different milling times.
DSC scans of the (Ti, Zr, Nb)50(Cu, Ni)40Al10 powder mixtures after milling for ≥24h (heating rate 20K/min).
XRD patterns of the powder mixtures with nominal composition of (Ti, Zr, Nb) x (Cu, Ni)90−x Al10 (10≤ x≤80) after milling for 48h.

TEM bright-field micrographs and the corresponding selected area electron diffraction patterns of the as-milled (Ti, Zr, Nb) x (Cu, Ni)90−x Al10 powder mixtures after milling for 48h: (a) x =50 and (b) x =80.
DSC scans of the (Ti, Zr, Nb) x (Cu, Ni)90−x Al10 powder mixtures (10≤ x ≤80) after milling for 48h (heating rate 20K/min).

Glass transition temperature, T g, onset temperature of crystallization, T x, and width of the supercooled liquid region, ΔT x, obtained from DSC scans as a function of (Ti, Zr, Nb) content for the mechanically alloyed (Ti, Zr, Nb) x (Cu, Ni)90−x Al10 powders (20≤ x ≤60) for 48h.

XRD patterns measured at room temperature for the mechanically alloyed (Ti, Zr, Nb) x (Cu, Ni)90−x Al10 (20≤ x≤60) powders for 48h after heating to 973K in the DSC at a heating rate of 20K/min followed by holding for 15min.

Calculated enthalpy of the pseudo-ternary (Ti, Zr, Nb) x (Cu, Ni)90−x Al10 alloy system. The calculated glass-forming range (GFR) and the experimentally determined GFR are plotted together for comparison. GFR A and B are estimated using the double tangent method and the general method, respectively.

article.ce:title: Amorphization in mechanically alloyed (Ti, Zr, Nb)–(Cu, Ni)–Al equiatomic alloys

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: FG Physikalische Metallkunde, FB 11 Material-und Geowissenschaften, Technische Universität Darmstadt, Petersenstraße 23, D-64287 Darmstadt, Germany

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Advanced Materials Engineerings, Sejong University, 98 Gunja-dong, Gwangjim-gu, Seoul 143-747, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phase formation by mechanical alloying in (Ti, Zr, Nb)–(Cu, Ni)–Al equiatomic alloys has been investigated. The structural features and the thermal stability were characterized by X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscopy and differential scanning calorimetry. An amorphous phase forms when the (Ti, Zr, Nb) content is between 20 and 60at.%. In contrast, solid solutions of Cu and (Nb, Zr) phase dominate in the final product with (Ti, Zr, Nb) content of 10 and ≥70at.%, respectively. All amorphous phases have a large supercooled liquid region of over 125K, indicating a high thermal stability against crystallization. Miedema's model is used to calculate the glass-forming range in this multicomponent alloy system. The calculated region coincides well with the experimental results.

ce:section.ce:para: In the recent decades, metallic glasses and their composites have attracted considerable attention due to their potential applications as new high-strength structural materials [1,2]. Metallic glasses can be formed in a large number of alloy systems based on some metallic elements by various methods, such as cooling from the melt, solid-state reaction and mechanical alloying [1,2]. In general, the conventional strategy for developing multicomponent metallic glasses is to select one main component based on its primarily excellent properties, such as low density, high mechanical properties and/or high corrosion resistance, and to use small atomic fractions of alloying additions to confer secondary properties and/or to enhance the glass-forming ability based on the confusion principle [2,3]. Such alloys have gradually developed from binary or ternary alloys into complex multicomponent systems starting from the corner or apexes of well known binary or ternary phase diagrams in order to obtain a high glass-forming ability, and to achieve the required balance of in-service properties for advanced engineering applications. For example, Fe-based metallic glasses have been developed from Fe–B binary alloys into Fe–Cr–Co–Mo–Mn–C–B–Y multicomponent alloys [4]. However, one of the results using the above-mentioned strategy to develop metallic glasses is that the easy glass-forming composition ranges are obviously limited in rather small composition ranges in the respective phase diagrams. For example, most ternary alloy systems have a very small composition range to form a glassy phase [5].

The conventional alloy development strategy leads to a large amount of knowledge about alloys based on one component, but to very little or no knowledge about alloys containing several main components in approximately equal proportions. Theories for the formation, structure and properties of crystalline phases are similarly restricted to alloys based on one or two main components [6]. Recently, Cantor et al. have developed a method for exploring a wide range of unknown multicomponent alloys by using equiatomic substitution [7], which begins with an alloy of interest and then individual alloy components are replaced with multicomponent equiatomic or near-equiatomic mixtures of chemically similar species. A series of novel high order alloys have been fabricated using equiatomic substitution for the central composition region of high order alloys using rapid solidification [8–10] and mechanical alloying techniques [11]. The supercooled liquid region, which is generally defined as the temperature interval (ΔT x) between the glass transition temperature (T g) and the onset temperature of crystallization (T x), of most of the high-order alloys was reported to be about 100K. For example, the ΔT x values of melt-spun (Ti, Zr, Hf)50(Cu, Ni, Ag)40Al10 [7] and (Ti, Zr, Hf)50(Cu, Ni, Co)40Al10 [10] glassy ribbons are 103 and 124K, respectively. Moreover, bulk Ti20Zr20Hf20Cu20Ni20 glass was reported to exhibit a high compressive fracture strength of 1920MPa [12].

Meanwhile, mechanical alloying of powders is a promising approach with several distinct advantages [13,14]. Firstly, mechanical alloying can form amorphous alloys at compositions difficult to alloy and amorphize using melt cooling methods. Secondly, large quantities of powders can be produced in the relatively inexpensive commercial mechanical alloying process, with the potential for the fabrication of near net shape components. In addition, mechanical alloying is extremely powerful in creating uniform dispersions of a strengthening/ductilizing second phase. It has been reported that amorphous phases can be formed in binary and multicomponent alloy systems [15–21] with a wide supercooled liquid region by mechanical alloying of metallic powders. The large supercooled liquid region opens up the possibility to fabricate truly bulk materials through powder processing and subsequent warm consolidation [21–25].

In comparison with the amorphous phase based on one or two main elements, very limited knowledge on the phase formation in equiatomic alloy systems is known. Miedema's model [26–28] can successfully predict the glass-forming range (GFR) in binary and ternary alloy systems, which agrees well with the experimental results [29–34]. However, to our knowledge, Miedema's model was not used so far to calculate the glass-forming range of multicomponent alloy systems composed of more than three components.

In the present work, the phase formation in a series of mechanically alloyed (Ti0.333Zr0.333Nb0.333) x (Cu0.5Ni0.5)90−x Al10 alloys was investigated. Additionally, Miedema's model was used to calculate the glass-forming range in this multicomponent alloy system consisting of six components. The calculated glass-forming range agrees well with the experimental result obtained by mechanical alloying.

Commercial Ti, Zr, Nb, Cu, Ni and Al elemental powders (Alfa Aesar) were used as starting materials. The as-received powders had a nominal weight purity of 99.9% or better and particle sizes of smaller than 45μm (−325 mesh). Powder mixtures with nominal compositions of (Ti0.333Zr0.333Nb0.333) x (Cu0.5Ni0.5)90−x Al10 (10≤ x ≤80, denoted as (Ti, Zr, Nb) x (Cu, Ni)90−x Al10 here after) were loaded together with hardened steel balls with diameter of 8mm in a hardened steel vial in an argon-filled M-Braun UNI-lab glove box with less than 1ppm O2 and H2O. A ball-to-powder weight ratio of about 10:1 was employed. The ball milling was performed with a Fritsch Pulverisette 5 planetary ball mill with a rotational speed of 200rpm.

The structural feature of the milled powders was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a Stoe diffractometer (Germany) with monochromated Cu Kα radiation (λ =0.1542nm). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations were carried out in a Philips CM 20 microscope. The glass transition and the crystallization behavior of the milled powders were examined using a Perkin-Elmer Diamond differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) under flowing purified argon with a heating rate of 20K/min. A second heating run under identical conditions was used to determine the baseline. To determine the onset temperature of the glass transition, T g, the as-prepared samples were pre-annealed at a set temperature of about T g +20K in the supercooled liquid region to reach a relaxed state for resolving the heat flow change in the DSC scan.

Fig. 1 shows the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the (Ti, Zr, Nb)50(Cu, Ni)40Al10 powder mixture after milling for different times. At the beginning (0h), the powder mixture is composed of the elemental components. After milling for 8h, alloying has occurred and a broadening and a reduction of intensity of the crystalline diffraction peaks are observed. Upon milling for 12h, a broad diffuse diffraction maximum is evident at 30°≤2θ ≤50°, which points to milling-induced amorphization, but diffraction peaks of residual crystallites of Nb and Zr are still visible. Upon further milling for 24h, the diffraction peaks of Nb disappear. Even after milling for 48h, diffraction peaks of Zr are still present. However, the intensity of the Zr peaks decreases compared with the sample milled for 24h. This is probably caused by the reduction of the volume fraction of the crystalline phase and/or further refinement of the crystallites [35]. The main amorphous scattering maximum is significantly asymmetric and the diffraction lines of unreacted Zr are still visible even after milling for 60h. After milling for 100h, crystallites of fcc structured (Nb, Zr) solid solution (Fm3m) with lattice parameter a =0.4392nm rather than an amorphous phase are present, which means dissolution of other components in this alloy into the (Nb, Zr) solid solution.

The DSC curves of the (Ti, Zr, Nb)50(Cu, Ni)40Al10 equiatomic powder after milling for 24h or longer time are displayed in Fig. 2 . As seen from the DSC curves, an endothermic signal associated with a glass transition and an exothermic peak due to crystallization of amorphous phase are evident for all the samples milled for ≥24h. For extended milling, the glass transition temperature remains almost the same (T g =720K), while the onset crystallization temperature (T x) increases from 886 (24h) to 902K (48h), which is owing to the appreciable change in the composition of the amorphous phase. Upon further extension of the milling time to 60h, the glass transition temperature and the onset crystallization temperature remain unchanged. In addition, the peak temperatures of the crystallization event (T p) for the samples milled for 48 and 60h are the same (T p =931K). This indicates that the mutual alloying of the elements and the crystal-to-amorphous transformation in the (Ti, Zr, Nb)50(Cu, Ni)40Al10 powder mixture is saturated at a steady state around 48h of milling. Therefore, in order to assure that the milling has proceeded sufficiently long for the samples to reach a steady state, but short enough to avoid excessive contamination from the milling media, the milling time for the (Ti, Zr, Nb) x (Cu, Ni)90−x Al10 powders with different composition was fixed at 48h.

Fig. 3 shows the XRD patterns of the powder mixtures with nominal composition of (Ti, Zr, Nb) x (Cu, Ni)90−x Al10 (10≤ x ≤80) after milling for 48h. Only broad diffraction lines of nanocrystalline Cu solid solution are found for x =10. A broad diffuse diffraction maximum at 30°≤2θ ≤50° is the main feature for the samples with a (Ti, Zr, Nb) content of 20–60at.%, suggesting that the as-milled powders (20≤ x ≤60) are predominantly amorphous. However, crystalline peaks of unreacted Zr solid solution are still identifiable in the XRD patterns of all the powders with x =20–60 even though the milling time has been extended to a steady state for the structural evolution (48h). In addition, the position of the first diffuse diffraction maximum gradually shifts to lower angles with increasing (Ti, Zr, Nb) content, which is consistent with the dissolution of a larger and larger amount of (Ti, Zr, Nb) with larger atomic size in the milling-induced amorphous phase. For x =70, the mechanical alloying induced crystal-to-amorphous transition is much more incomplete. The final product of the x =70 alloy contains unreacted Ti, Zr and Nb solid solutions besides only a small volume fraction of amorphous phase. When the (Ti, Zr, Nb) content is further increased to x =80 (Nb, Zr), solid solution with fcc structure (Fm3m) is predominantly formed in the final product.

Fig. 4 shows the TEM bright-field micrographs and the corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns (inset) of the milled x =50 and 80 alloy powder mixtures milled for 48h. The microstructure of the x =50 alloy (Fig. 4(a)) exhibits some bright particles of the unreacted Zr solid solution with a grain size of 10–20nm embedded in the contrastless amorphous matrix. In comparison, as seen in Fig. 4(b), only fcc Nb(Zr) solid solution phase with a grain size of 10–200nm is formed in the x =80 alloy.

Fig. 5 displays the DSC scans in the mode of continuous heating with a heating rate of 20K/min for the (Ti, Zr, Nb) x (Cu, Ni)90−x Al10 powders (10≤ x ≤80) after milling for 48h. When the (Ti, Zr, Nb) content is in the range of 20–60at.%, an endothermic signal associated with the glass transition and an exothermic reaction owing to crystallization of the amorphous phase are evident in the DSC scans. The crystallization of the amorphous phase proceeds through one single-step event for 20≤ x ≤50. However, with further increasing the (Ti, Zr, Nb) content to x =60, the single-step crystallization event changes to a two-step process. For x =70, only a very small exothermic peak is visible in the DSC curve. In contrast, no exothermic peak is visible for the samples with (Ti, Zr, Nb) contents of 10 and 80at.%, which indicates that no amorphous is formed in the two alloys. The glass transition temperatures, onset crystallization temperatures and the extension of the supercooled liquid region of the alloys with 20≤ x ≤60 are plotted in Fig. 6 . As the (Ti, Zr, Nb) content increases, both T g and T x gradually shift to higher temperatures. T g and T x increase from 655 and 780K for x =20 to 720 and 902K for x =50, respectively, resulting in an extension of the supercooled liquid region from 125K for x =20 to 183K for x =50. With further increasing the (Ti, Zr, Nb) content to x =60, both T g and T x drop to 672 and 839K, respectively. However, the supercooled liquid region is not remarkably reduced.

To identify the structural changes related to the crystallization event, the powders (20≤ x ≤60) milled for 48h were continuously heated in the DSC to 973K with a heating rate of 20K/min and holding for 15min, and then cooled down to room temperature at 100K/min for subsequent XRD measurements. The XRD patterns are shown in Fig. 7 . For the amorphous powders with a (Ti, Zr, Nb) content of 20 and 40at.%, the amorphous phase transformed into the cubic Al(Nb, Zr)Ni2 and Nb2Ni phases. When the (Ti, Zr, Nb) content increased to 50at.%, a small volume fraction of Nb(Ti) solid solution appears in the crystallized powders besides cubic Al(Nb, Zr)Ni2 and Nb2Ni phases. Moreover, the volume fraction of Nb2Ni increases when the (Ti, Zr, Nb) content increases from 30 to 50at.%. For x =60, the volume fraction of the Nb(Ti) solid solution increases while the volume fraction of Nb2Ni decreases.

It is well known that Miedema's model [26–28] can be used to predict the glass-forming range of binary and ternary alloy systems [30–34]. In general, by comparing the enthalpy difference between the formation enthalpy of the amorphous phase (ΔH amorphous) and that of the solid solution (ΔH s.s.) or intermetallic compounds (ΔH compounds), the glass-forming range can be estimated. That is, if the enthalpy of the formation of the amorphous phase is lower than that of the solid solution or the intermetallic compounds, the amorphous phase is preferentially formed; otherwise, when considering metastable equilibrium conditions, the solid solution or intermetallic compounds easily form (“general method”). Sometimes, the glass-forming range can also be estimated using the double tangent method, i.e. using two common tangents between the enthalpy curves of the amorphous phase and the competitive phases such as the solid solutions and the intermetallic compounds [36]. The compositions where the formation enthalpy curve of the amorphous phase attains the lowest absolute values then define the glass-forming range. In contrast, the compositions between the common tangents give an estimate for the two-phase region where the amorphous phase coexists with crystalline phases. In the present work, the (Ti, Zr, Nb)–(Cu, Ni)–Al alloys can be considered as a pseudo-ternary alloy system. (Ti, Zr, Nb) may be regarded as A, (Cu, Ni) as B and Al as C, respectively. The corresponding parameters of each pseudo-component (i.e. A, B and C) can be calculated using the confusion principle. As seen from the XRD results shown in Fig. 3, the competitive phases in the course of the formation of the amorphous phase are solid solutions (i.e. Cu and (Nb, Zr) solid solutions). Therefore, only the formation enthalpies of the amorphous phase and the solid solutions are considered rather than that of intermetallic compounds to estimate the glass-forming range in the present work. At the same time, the glass-forming range is estimated using the above-mentioned general method along with the double tangents method. Both calculated glass-forming ranges were compared with the experimental results.

In a ternary system A–B–C, the formation enthalpies of the amorphous phase are given by the equations:

The structural contribution to the enthalpy of solid solution, arising from the difference between the valences and crystal structures of the solute and the solvent, is expected to have only a minor effect and can be almost negligible in determining the glass-forming composition range [28,39] when compared with the elastic energy contribution. Also, it is difficult to calculate this structural contribution in the multicomponent alloy systems and so it was not considered in the present calculations. Accordingly, in this work the specific crystal structure of the solid solution was not taken into account when estimating the formation enthalpies in Miedema's model [28]. Hence, the formation enthalpies of a solid solution phase can be derived from:
(6)

i is the molar volume of pure component i, ϕ * the parameters are the electronegativity (in V) and n ws the parameters are the electron densities at the Wigner–Seitz cell boundary (in density units). The parameter P 0 has been found empirically to be 1.5 [40]. All numerical values of the parameters are available in Ref. [26].

The calculated formation enthalpies of the amorphous phase and the solid solution for the (Ti, Zr, Nb)–(Cu, Ni)–Al pseudo-ternary alloy system is illustrated in Fig. 8 . The calculated formation enthalpies of the solid solution in the (Ti, Zr, Nb)–(Cu, Ni)–Al alloy system is symmetric. Hence, the (Ti, Zr, Nb)50(Cu, Ni)40Al10 alloy has the lowest solid solution enthalpy. The calculated formation enthalpies of the amorphous phase in the (Ti, Zr, Nb)–(Cu, Ni)–Al alloy system is asymmetric, and the lower enthalpy lies towards the (Cu, Ni) side. Note that the formation enthalpies of the solid solutions and the amorphous phase at the terminal compositions, such as (Cu, Ni)90Al10 and (Ti, Zr, Nb)90Al10, are not zero but have negative enthalpy values, which should reach zero for the enthalpies of the terminal composition in the binary alloy system extrapolated according to Miedema's model. Similar results have also been reported in other ternary alloy systems [29,34]. The difference between the calculation and the extrapolation of the enthalpy values for the amorphous phase and the solid solution is related to the SRO parameter and to a contribution for the amorphization of the pure components, see Eq. (4). Comparing with the formation enthalpies of the solid solution and those of the amorphous phase in this (Ti, Zr, Nb)–(Cu, Ni)–Al pseudo-ternary alloy system, the calculated glass-forming composition range corresponds to a (Ti, Zr, Nb) content of about from 21 to 51at.% using the double tangent method (see GFR A in Fig. 8). In contrast, when using the general method, the calculated GFR is estimated to be 12≤ x ≤63 (see GFR B in Fig. 8). From the experiments, an amorphous phase was formed for the powder mixtures with a (Ti, Zr, Nb) content of 20–60at.%, while predominantly crystalline material was formed in the powders with a (Ti, Zr, Nb) content of 10 and ≥70at.%. Comparing with the calculated the experimental glass-forming range, the calculated GFR using the double tangent method is much narrower than the experimental result, whereas the calculated GFR using the general method is in good accord with the experimental results. Therefore, Miedema's model can be successfully applied to calculate the glass-forming range in multicomponent alloy systems composed of more than three components. However, further work is necessary to fully understand the details of the amorphization reaction in such systems.

It is interesting to note that the phase transition in equiatomic (Ti, Zr, Nb)50(Cu, Ni)40Al10 is strongly dependent on the milling time, as shown in Fig. 1. A crystal-to-glass transformation occurs in the early stage of the milling, resulting in the formation of an amorphous phase (elements→amorphous phase). In contrast, a glass-to-crystal transformation happens when the powder mixture experiences a longer milling time, leading to the formation of the fcc (Nb, Zr) solid solution phase (amorphous→(Nb, Zr) solid solution). A similar phenomenon was also found in the Ni–Zr binary alloy system [41–43] under ball milling. For the milling of Al–Cu–Mn powder mixtures [42], longer milling time results in the crystallization of an amorphous phase into a quasicrystalline phase. For the milling of Ni68Zr32 alloy at rather high milling intensity 7 [43], an amorphous phase was formed after milling for 12h, whereas partial crystallization of the amorphous phase into the Ni3Zr compound was observed after 60h, which was assumed to be due to a strong temperature increase during further milling. However, for our work, the final product of x =50 after 100h is the fcc (Nb, Zr) solid solution phase (Pm3m) (Fig. 1), which is significantly different from the crystallized production with a mixture of Al(Nb, Zr)Ni2, Nb2Ni and bcc Nb solid solution phase (Im3m), as shown in Fig. 7. Following the procedure proposed by Schwarz and Koch [44], it is assumed that the particles are deformed by localized shear during a collision between the balls. The peak temperature reached within the powder particles during milling can be estimated as follows. The normal stress developed by the head-on collision of two balls is
σ n
=
ρ b
v s
v r
, where ρ b =7.8gcm−3 is the density of the steel balls,
v s
= 5800
m
s
− 1
the speed of a longitudinal wave in steel, and
v r
= 4.7
m
s
− 1
is the relative velocity of the balls in this work. The shear process lasts for
Δ
t = 2 d /
v s
, where d =0.8cm is the diameter of the steel balls and the energy flux dissipated on the glide plane is
F =
σ n
v r
. The resulting temperature increase ΔT by the heat flux F/2 for time Δt is:
(11)
Δ
T = F
Δ
t
π
K 0
ρ p
c p
1 / 2
.
For the powder density ρ p and the specific heat c
p , we used weighted averages of the pure component, giving ρ p =7.08gcm−3 and c
p
=434Jkg−1 K−1 for x =50. With K 0 =5.0Wm−1 K−1 we obtain ΔT =75K. Even taking the overall temperature of the milling container of about 393K into account [41], the total peak temperature of the powder particles will reach 468K, which is about 250K lower than the glass transition temperature of 719K for x =50. So the rise of the temperature during milling will not lead to partial crystallization of the amorphous phases. Actually, the possible phase transition upon milling are very complicated, and the driving forces and the mechanisms of amorphization are still controversial. The reason why the amorphous phase transforms into a metastable fcc (Nb, Zr) solid solution phase rather than into bcc or hcp equilibrium phase(s) after longer milling time is not clear yet. However, it is well known that mechanical alloying is a non-equilibrium process and metastable phases can be formed under such conditions [13]. For example, α-Zr will transform to the high temperature ω-Zr phase in binary Fe–Zr alloys under mechanical alloying [45].

The phase formation in multicomponent (Ti0.333Zr0.333Nb0.333) x (Cu0.5Ni0.5)90−x Al10 alloys was investigated through mechanical alloying. An amorphous phase is the predominant product for the powders with a (Ti, Zr, Nb) content of 20–60at.%, while solid solutions of Cu and (Nb, Zr) phase dominate in the final powder with a (Ti, Zr, Nb) content of 10 and ≥70at.%, respectively. All the mechanical alloying induced amorphous powders have a wide supercooled liquid region larger than 125K. Miedema's model can be successfully applied to calculate the glass-forming range in multicomponent alloy system composed of more than three components, and the calculated glass-forming range agrees well with the experimental results.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Dr. Hai-Bo Lu for stimulating discussions and J.C. Jaud for technical assistance with X-ray diffraction. Financial support by the EU via the RTN-network on “ductile bulk metallic glass composites” (MRTN-CT-2003-504692) is gratefully acknowledged. L.C. Zhang, P. Yu and W.Y. Zhang are indebted to the financial support of the Alexander von Humboldt Foundation.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nonequilibrium Phase Diagrams of Ternary Amorphous Alloys
An Introduction to Materials Engineering and Science
Cohesion in Metals: Transition Metal Alloys
